lines.text: "It's a new science." That's Ines Martel, in the dimmest corner of the Dáinn. Welcome to Sci-Fi.
Welcome beyond the available vocabulary.
"Smile. Nod. Take notes. Or pretend to. That's what I did." Someone in the future is confessing they faked comprehension. Which is exactly what reading this chapter is going to feel like.
Let's start with Martel and the perceptual net. Because that's the conceptual scaffolding for everything that follows.

The net is straight Immanuel Kant in fleet uniform. We don't see the world. We see what our perceptual apparatus is built to catch. "The net contains holes, allowing most of the universe to pass through unnoticed by our consciousness."
That's also what cognitive neuroscience has been saying for fifteen years.
The Fold tears, or interweaves the perceptual nets. The Bris was exposed too long. And the metaphor she reaches for is *entangled*. She's using the language of quantum entanglement.

It's also a clean trauma-research framing in disguise. Bessel van der Kolk's whole project — *the body keeps the score* because the mind's narrative net couldn't catch what happened. The signal sits unprocessed, somewhere below language.
So the Bris crew is — at one register — collectively traumatized. At another, in genuine first-contact with something that doesn't fit any frame they brought with them.
And those two registers refuse to separate. They keep collapsing into each other. Which I think is the move Nyland is making with this whole arc.
Cut to the Bris bridge. A cathedral made of nutrient paste and glowing algae. Gupta on the floor painting symbols. This is ritual space.

Sacred geography? The bridge is now a church. The viewport — the window onto outside — is deliberately covered. Which Gupta makes explicit at the end: "Do you still think that window ever looked out?"
That's a Platonic cave. Plato says the world we see is shadow. Gupta agrees and adds: "You look at a shadow on the wall and call it the Self."
And then he and Adrian have what is essentially the Descartes-versus-Whitehead debate, on the floor of a painted cathedral, while the ship's algae glows.

Adrian opens with dualism. Body is biology. But consciousness — what is that *made of*? That's the hard problem. The mind-body problem. The one nobody's solved. Gupta states there is only mind. The body is just a thought.

Gupta's response is process-philosophy so pure it could be quoted from Whitehead. "The body only appears solid from our subjective experience. In truth, it is a slow and beautiful thought, unfolding steadily—like a song, or a painting."
The body isn't a substance — it's an event. A standing wave.
And then he twists the knife. "One thought does not recognize itself in the other." That dissolves continuity of self entirely. Does the one brushstroke recognise itself in the next?
But who then is the painter?
Gupta presents himself as the great Artist. Adrian's confusion is — honestly — beautiful. He reaches for Descartes. *Cogito, ergo sum*. The original move against doubt of the self.

It works for half a second. Then he subverts it. The next thought arrives and doesn't recognize the cogito as its predecessor. "Do I exist when I am *not* thinking?" Vedanta has been asking that for two thousand years. Where *are* you in dreamless sleep?

And clinically, this is depersonalization happening in real time. "The falling accelerated, and he spun. There was no more center to hold on to." That's a phenomenological description of dissociation. Loss of the unified observer.

So he reaches for the stone. e-human, possibly "worked by a lost technology." He clutches it the way Winnicott described a child clutching the transitional object — the thing you hold to remember the world is solid and you are continuous with yesterday's you.
And the stone betrays him. "Adrian was no longer certain whether he was thinking about the stone… or whether the stone was trying to understand itself through him."

That's a hard pivot into panpsychism. "A stone that thinks it is a stone is not just a stone." That line is going to live with me. Spinoza would nod. Schelling would weep. Adrian's last anchor has just told him it's also a subject.
Nyland warned us we would get 'spin sick'. Meanwhile, Stacey is at the comms station fixing nothing because nothing is broken. And Gupta tells her: "speak less, paint more."
He drags a smudged finger across her console. She rolls her eyes. She traces the shape. Uplink established. "Nature opens its eyes to those who paint." And argument against Newton.
And it's also a psychological surrender. She had to relinquish control, agency, there it is again, to make the thing work.
Ok, I'm spinning. So, let's jump to Hale who's taking Lena for a spin in the Stingray.
A jab for a jab. This is Hale's way of getting back at Lena for knocking him out. The orgy scene.

But Hale is also lowering his shield when asked about his relationship with Stacey Kiran. "She's the one I want to keep around. And that terrifies me." That's the chapter's emotional thesis. Hale is admitting he might lose himself to someone who could actually see him.
Intimacy and the loss of self are the same problem in different costumes. Hale can handle the Stringray. But his ego can't handle being loved.
Then the call between Ben and Gupta. Captain to captain. And here Nyland sets up the actual incommensurability problem.
Yes. They're speaking from inside two different ontologies. Humans have integrity, autonomy, the right not to be violated.

Gupta sees it this way. "A seed does not have integrity like a stone. Its purpose is to break open and become something greater." He's translating Ben's vocabulary back through Buddhism. Integrity isn't preservation — it's faithful unfolding. The seed is most itself when it stops being a seed.
Ben says "violated." Gupta says "ripened."
And Gupta closes with: "The ocean cannot be contained in a jar."  And then, "No, Ben. I have a bigger jar!"
The whole chapter is asking us: is Gupta an embodied deity? Or just a psychotic captain? Peak mystical experience and acute psychosis are very hard to tell apart from the outside.
Well. Let's find out how it unfolds.
